sentences.text: Where I learn?
There in the camp.
You know, in the evening, we go together, you know, and some, uh, volunteer teacher from the camp, you know, they teach us Russian.
No, only these immigrants, nothing, uh, different, not a Russian.
The Russian people, the local people, they distance from us, you know?
You know, they was very afraid to make friendship with foreigners, you know?
They're Russian.
They don't-- was very afraid to come in contact with foreigners, you know?
In the authority?
They were not actually difficult.
It's why we have the documents.
We are-- permission.
No-- no.
It was-- different was when we work, you know, as help worker, you know?
On the farm, you know?
They took-- talk with us-- no, not when there was the other one, only when he was alone.
No weakness, you know?
There were always this, you know, from the interior police, you know?
There was--
So what I said in 1942, I got the permission, go to the army.
No-- no.
I don't show him the documents.
I-- I tell I am from Czechoslovakia, you know.
The documents, I show only the interior police, not the private people.
In a-- Jewish [INAUDIBLE], started in--
--Tajikistan.
There was a lot Polish people, Polish Jewish people.
And there was a lot people that throw away from the Baltic states, you know?
When the Soviets occupied the Baltic states, Litwa, Latvia, you know, they threw away first the Jewish all in Asia, you know?
No.
No, not so.
They-- they came to work hard, you know?
No, I don't remember to.
No, in this-- I must tell, in this, I speak about the Russia.
I never-- nobody hit me, all the time, no different.
You know, there was a special-- when they started to you-- you know, the [INAUDIBLE], interior police.
You must stay on the corridor with the face to the wall and their hands on the wall, you know?
It was all.
No, I say in Tajikistan, it was within Jewish prisoner, you know, what about the religion.
So there was a lot.
No, you can't-- you can't see, is-- he's a Tajik or he's a Jew.
The wearing.
You know, they don't work.
Yes.
I haven't seen in Tajikistan a synagogue.
I never have seen.
I don't know.
I don't-- I was always in the brick factory, [INAUDIBLE], not in the town.
Yes, more or less.
No, no mix.
The Polish people, when there was the Polish army, you know, they go to the Polish army, you know?
No, there was mixed.
They was from Poland, you know, a lot.
So I start at the army.
I got back my-- these.
In 1943, started-- we go to the front, yes?
And after, come back to Novo [INAUDIBLE].
What?
To the front?
They take us with the train, you know, and walking, again.
So we started in-- near Kharkov, you know?
In the army, in the Czech army, the first beginning, we have been over thousand people.
From these thousand people-- I will remember the place.
We're near to Kharkov-- was nearly 700 Jewish, you know?
All Jewish, Czechoslovak Jewish.
And their died, I don't know, few hundreds, you know?
'43-- it was spring.
We go to the front, you know, and we helped liberate Kiev, the town Kiev on the Ukraine, yes, the Czechoslovak tankist.
It's difficult to tell, you know?
You stay in the tanks and move and move and move.
You don't see nothing.
You use this.
No, it's very difficult.
Now what happens, all the Soviets, they take the people, the ill people, inside to Russia.
So I was afraid when they took me to Russia, I never come back.
So in the night, you know, I run away from this lazarette back to the Czechs.
We'd [INAUDIBLE] with this.
What?
No, when we go with the army, you know--
--there was small camps, you know?
German these, yes?
Nobody there, I tell you, I will.
So we go to the-- from Poland down to Bukovina and Czernowitz.
We stayed there, and from there go to the [?
Carpaths, ?] you know, and cross the [?
Carpathia, ?] yes, with all small this.
Yes, to the [?
Carpaths, ?] to Czechoslovakia, Slovakia.
And we stayed a tankist near Kosice, you know?
How you call this?
Kezmarok, you know?
About Auschwitz?
I was a few-- few hours only.
I can tell you one thing.
My late wife, she was same, in the army, you know?
When we came there, she took my hand and told me, Albert, you know, I am from an Orthodox Jewish family, and my parents.
Now when I see these chimneys and see just what I can see, I hope my parents forgive me.
I don't believe in God.
Say [INAUDIBLE], I don't say the word.
Yes.
So I found nobody was there.
My late wife, you know-- I was officer-- goes to the commander, you know, and asks him for a lorry.
So what she have done, she took some people from the town where she was born and from Jelenia, and took on the lorry and took them back to Slovakia.
What?
What I like?
First of all, we have enough to eat, you know?
The uniform and the transport, it was from Britain.
So we have the battle dress-- British battle dress in the army, you know?
Normally, these prisoners, you know, some soldiers, they took it, give it to the Russian.
Know to-- to took it to the Russian, you need two, three days to take them and bring him back, then come back.
No, when I have seen it was an SS, you know, or an SR men, there's mostly lie.
So I take this soldier and tell him, take these men to the Russian, give it up.
No, you must be back in a half hour.
Janko.
[INAUDIBLE] Janko was my commander.
No, Czech.
And he took his life in 1968 when the Russia liberated us the second time.
When I [INAUDIBLE] my wife, I-- I leave my wife in the, uh, farm.
Met her.
No, she was married, you know?
So I started a friendship.
No, her husband die-- died in the war, in the first front.
No, no, no.
She stayed in the back.
She come after-- after Kiev, when we have been, you know?
She was still in Buzuluk, you know, or the-- the part of the army was there.
So she stayed there.
Yes, at Kiev, they make a big celebration.
They give the-- there was three-- I don't know how you say in English-- three groups, it was the big tanks, yes, the small tanks, and the other group, you know?
So all these commanders from the three groups, they got to the big Russian orders, you know?
And the commander got same, a Lenin order.
And I got the first War Cross, [POINTS], this one, yes, the Czechoslovak War-- and again, I go one higher with this.
So I finished the war, what I say, in--
Sorry?
In the brigade was Jewish people.
And what I forgot in between, you know, to Czechoslovakia belonged the so-called Carpatho-Rusland.
This was a part of Czechoslovakia.
And in this war time before, when the Russian did, they took away this Carpatho-Rus, you know, and took it in the forced labor camps.
And in '42-- no, [INAUDIBLE]-- yes, '42, they liberated them and they come in the Czechoslovak army.
So there was a lot of, uh, Czechoslovak Ukraine, yes?
Zakarpatska Ukraine they call it.
So there was Czechs, Slovaks, and this.
And after-- I don't remember a bit.
In Slovakia have the Slovak army.
And there was, uh, fight against the Russian.
No, the same-- they run away and come to us, to the Czechoslovak army.
So there was a lot of Slovaks too.
Sorry?
In Czech, everything in Czech.
Not in Slovak, all in Czech.
